locked_facts: Voltage LEADS current by π/2 (90°) — equivalently, current LAGS voltage by 90°
X_L increases with frequency — inductor blocks high-frequency AC more
Average power dissipated in pure inductor = 0 (energy stored and returned)
Inductor opposes CHANGE in current — not current itself
truth_statements: Voltage leads current by exactly 90° for a pure inductor
Inductive reactance X_L is proportional to frequency — doubles when frequency doubles
A pure inductor dissipates zero average power
The back-EMF of the inductor opposes changes in current, causing the phase lag

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show V peaking at time t₁ and I not peaking until time t₂ = t₁ + T/4. The current peak is delayed by exactly a quarter cycle. This is not arbitrary — it is because the inductor always generates back-EMF that opposes the rate of change of current, and the current through an inductor cannot change instantaneously.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Draw a vertical dashed line at V_peak. Then show I_peak occurring T/4 later — another vertical dashed line. Label the gap as T/4 = π/2 phase delay.

simulation_states.label: AC source connected to inductor
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Circuit with AC source and inductor. Equation v = V_m sin(ωt) on the source.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: An inductor stores energy in its magnetic field — it does not dissipate it. This fundamental difference from resistor will cause a phase shift.

simulation_states.label: V and I waveforms — I lags by 90°
simulation_states.what_student_sees: v(t) in orange, i(t) in blue. The current waveform is clearly shifted 90° to the right (delayed). When V peaks, I = 0. When V = 0 (and falling), I peaks.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: I lags V by 90° means: when voltage reaches maximum, current is only just crossing zero. The current is a quarter cycle behind the voltage.

simulation_states.label: Why the lag — the physical mechanism
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Animation showing: as V rises, the inductor generates back-EMF resisting the current increase. Current builds up slowly. When V starts falling, current is still rising. The opposition to CHANGE causes the delay.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The inductor does not oppose current — it opposes CHANGE in current. This is the physical origin of the phase lag.

simulation_states.label: X_L increases with frequency
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider for frequency. As frequency increases, X_L = ωL increases, I_m = V_m/X_L decreases. High frequency AC is blocked more by inductor.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Inductor is a high-frequency blocker. At DC (f=0), X_L = 0 — inductor is a short circuit. At very high frequency, X_L → ∞ — inductor is an open circuit.

trigger_keywords: I aage hota hai L mein
common_misconceptions.student_belief: In an inductor, current leads voltage (confusing direction of lead/lag)
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: In an inductor, VOLTAGE leads CURRENT by 90°. Equivalently, CURRENT lags VOLTAGE by 90°. The mnemonic: ELI (E = voltage, L = inductor, I = current → V leads I in L).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the two waveforms with a clear arrow indicating: V peaks FIRST (earlier in time), I peaks LATER. Label: V leads I, or equivalently, I lags V.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: An inductor dissipates power because it draws current from the source
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A pure inductor dissipates zero average power. It draws energy from the source in one quarter cycle and returns it completely in the next quarter cycle. Net energy transfer is zero.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show P(t) = v(t) × i(t) for the inductor. It oscillates between positive and negative symmetrically — positive when inductor absorbs energy, negative when it returns energy. Average = exactly zero.

legend.meaning: Voltage leads current by 90° in inductor
Mnemonic: E(voltage) leads I(current) in L(inductor)
